chapters.title: Breaking the repeating pattern
chapters.text:
Have you ever felt trapped in relationships that left you anxious, confused, or miserable? Jessica Baum lived that cycle. Before becoming a licensed therapist, she spent years pursuing men who could not meet her emotional needs.

In her twenties she dated someone career-obsessed who gradually pulled away. His distance triggered abandonment panic so severe she landed in the hospital. Her first marriage paired her with a partner afraid of intimacy — whenever closeness grew, he withdrew, leaving her isolated and frantic.

Identify your attachment style and heal yourself first to break free from toxic relationship cycles.

Baum lacked language for what she felt. Learning she had an anxious attachment style — a pattern that drove her toward emotionally unavailable partners — transformed her outlook. Understanding the dynamic helped her build a secure, fulfilling relationship with someone new.

Healing does not happen by accident. It requires deliberate work to understand attachment wounds and learn self-trust. Sound familiar? Let us examine your patterns together.

chapters.title: Where attachment begins
chapters.text:
Before exploring attachment theory, try a quick self-check. Lower one finger for each "yes:"

• Do you constantly think about your partner, even at the expense of other interests?
• Do you get anxious when they don't reply to your messages quickly?
• Do you feel disappointed when your partner doesn't meet your expectations?
• Do you try to reconnect immediately after a conflict, unable to let it go?
• Do you check your partner's social media or messages to ensure they aren't hiding something?

Four or more fingers down suggests an anxious attachment style. Here is what that means.

Psychologist John Bowlby developed attachment theory in the 1950s. Humans are wired to connect from birth, beginning with the primary caregiver. Infants cannot grasp how formative that bond is. Caregiver responsiveness to early needs shapes relational security for decades.

Three main styles emerge from childhood: anxious, avoidant, and secure.

1. Anxious people fear abandonment and obsess over relationships to feel safe.
2. Avoidant people prize independence and distrust intimacy because early emotional needs went unmet.
3. Secure people form relationships while maintaining healthy boundaries and outside connections.

Researchers Cindy Hazan and Phillip Shaver later estimated roughly 56% of adults are secure, 25% anxious, and 19% avoidant. Childhood patterns echo loudly in romance.

Healing comes when you learn to balance your needs and respect your boundaries.

Recognizing your style is step one toward healthier bonds. Self-knowledge turns relationships into opportunities for growth. True security in love starts within.

Once you name the pattern, you stop mistaking anxiety for love and start choosing partners who can actually meet you there.

chapters.title: Why toxic love hooks us
chapters.text:
Baum discovered her anxious attachment by listening to her body. When partners ghosted or withdrew, panic hit: sinking stomach, racing heart, desperate attempts to reconnect — repeated texts, needless apologies. Her nervous system was signaling danger and abandonment.

Attachment styles shape the body, not just the mind. Stephen Porges' polyvagal theory describes how neuroception — constant environmental scanning — governs safety. In the ventral state we breathe slowly, faces soften, and connection feels natural.

Your body is often smarter than you; listen to it for signals when you need them.

Perceived threat triggers fight-or-flight. For anxiously attached people, a delayed text can be enough. Hearts race, voices tighten, and chasing or demanding reassurance begins — which, in Baum's experience, pushed partners further away, locking both into fear and withdrawal.

If this sounds familiar, it was not imaginary. The nervous system reacts before rational thought catches up. Next time, observe bodily signals first. A pounding heart need not dictate your actions.

Try "heart awareness:"

1. Ask, "How am I feeling right now?" and focus on your chest.
2. Notice breathing — calm or rushed? Slow it deliberately.
3. Inhale for five counts, exhale for seven. Let the stomach expand.
4. Check posture. Roll shoulders back to open the chest.
5. Imagine breath washing over your heart, creating space to open up.

This practice interrupts the automatic chase for reassurance and gives your nervous system a chance to settle before you act.

chapters.title: Meeting your younger self
chapters.text:
Childhood may feel distant, yet everyone carries a younger self — the "Little Me" — into adulthood. This inner child absorbed emotions and experiences while growing up. Repeated moments when needs went unmet — a parent ignoring cries, for instance — become core wounds stored in the body and replayed in adult relationships.

In anxious attachment the amygdala grows hypersensitive to abandonment cues, producing outsized reactions. Avoidant attachment often stems from emotional neglect, building walls that shut down intimacy when threatened.

Baum's patient Susan illustrates the pattern with her partner Dan. Susan asked him repeatedly to help with dishes after cooking. When he forgot, her Little Me felt unappreciated and abandoned. Fear of driving Dan away kept her silent until frustration exploded one evening into a full argument — classic anxious-attachment behavior where avoiding confrontation ultimately destroys the bond.

Take time to reflect on and recognize your core wounds.

Healing begins by connecting with Little Me — acknowledging wounds and the beliefs they forged. Real recovery means facing old fears the younger self still carries.

Try this core-wound exercise:

1. Write your core wound (e.g., "I will always be left").
2. Recall a childhood moment that first triggered it.
3. Name the belief it created (e.g., "My needs don't matter").
4. Note how it appears in current relationships.
5. Observe physical sensations and emotions that surface (tight chest, sadness, etc.).

Core wounds lose power when you stop treating present-day conflict as proof that childhood fears were right all along.

chapters.title: Why opposites collide
chapters.text:
Why do opposite attachment styles magnetize each other? Together they trigger deepest fears, creating a push-pull cycle. Anxious partners crave closeness; avoidant partners feel suffocated and retreat. Both end up feeling unseen.

Anxious individuals deploy activating strategies — behaviors to restore connection when the relationship feels threatened. Abandonment terror fuels them.

Common activating strategies:

• Flooding a partner with texts or calls after no reply.
• Tracking response times and delaying replies to "even the score."
• Over-apologizing to prevent conflict.
• Insisting on long emotional talks until reassurance arrives.
• Monitoring social media for signs of betrayal.

Avoidant partners use deactivating strategies to limit vulnerability: ghosting for days, dodging emotional talks, avoiding physical affection, citing work or travel for space, or ending things when intimacy deepens.

Each side reinforces the other's fears.

Become aware of your relationship strategies to break the cycle of unhealthy communication.

Anxiously attached people are especially vulnerable to narcissists who breadcrumb — offering just enough attention to maintain control without consistent care. Intermittent reinforcement keeps hope alive, like gambling for a jackpot that never pays out.

Attachment styles are not destiny. As we heal and recognize patterns, the pull toward toxic dynamics weakens. Secure, stable relationships that meet emotional needs become the priority.

Seeing activating and deactivating strategies clearly is often the breakthrough that stops the same painful argument from replaying indefinitely.

chapters.title: Guardians and caregivers within
chapters.text:
Your Little Me is not alone. Inner Protectors and Inner Nurturers — internal voices that can guide healing and restore balance — keep them company.

Inner Protectors formed to shield you from emotional harm. They sound like harsh critics or defense mechanisms preventing repetition of childhood pain. A child punished for mistakes might develop perfectionism as a Protector, blocking vulnerability in relationships.

To connect with Inner Protectors:

• Listen for "should" and "shouldn't" thoughts.
• Notice recurring themes — perfectionism, abandonment terror.
• Thank them for keeping you safe and affirm that healing is underway.

Inner Nurturers embody compassionate, loving parts of yourself — voices internalized from people, places, or experiences that offered unconditional support.

Examples include:

• A parent or mentor who made you feel valued.
• A friend who consistently showed empathy.
• A spiritual figure providing purpose and calm.

Balance your Inner Protectors and Inner Nurturers to create space for your emotional healing and growth.

To cultivate Inner Nurturers:

1. Picture your chosen Nurturer and feel their warmth.
2. Let kindness flow in; notice physical and emotional effects.
3. Ask if they have a message. Listen for words, sensations, or feelings.
4. If a Protector intrudes with fear, let the Nurturer reassure gently.
5. Thank both voices for their roles — healing takes time.

Labeling emotions also helps Little Me. Dan Siegel's research shows naming feelings — "I feel sad" or "I feel anxious" — engages the logical brain, aiding processing and calming the nervous system.

Inner Protectors and Inner Nurturers are not abstract ideas. They are practical tools for responding to fear with compassion instead of criticism.

chapters.title: Becoming self-full
chapters.text:
Healing means becoming self-full — building a healthy relationship with yourself where you honor your own needs instead of abandoning them for others. That requires balancing self-care with genuine connection.

All your feelings need to know they matter, all our feelings need to be heard. ~ Jessica Baum

Anxiously attached people especially struggle here, often prioritizing partners' emotions over their own — a habit rooted in childhood. A child who suppresses sadness to prevent parental withdrawal learns to disconnect from personal needs, then seeks validation from romantic partners as adults.

Without self-fullness, constant attention feels like security.

Healthy boundaries counter the pattern. Boundaries are not ultimatums or walls — they are clear, respectful statements of need.

Starting steps:

• Pause before decisions to check alignment with your needs.
• Examine emotions first — unresolved wounds may be driving fear-based choices.
• Listen to bodily signals about right and wrong.
• Communicate limits calmly, not from guilt or fear.

Self-fullness also means defining non-negotiables — lines you will not cross, from zero tolerance for violence to shared values. Knowing them builds interdependent relationships where both partners grow while respecting limits.

Did you know? Mirror-neuron research shows how deeply we absorb others' states. If a partner is stressed, you may feel it as your own — the same applies between parents and children.

Becoming self-full means noticing that absorption and deciding which feelings are truly yours to carry.

chapters.title: Moving from anxious to secure
chapters.text:
As self-fullness grows, secure relationships become possible. After the honeymoon phase, partners reveal authentic selves and old wounds. Then the real work begins: how do we move forward?

Secure connection demands ongoing effort:

• Accept that different histories shape each partner's reactions.
• Build empathy and learn each other's triggers.
• Approach conflict with curiosity, not blame.

Secure couples still fight — they simply repair ruptures effectively.

Proactive conversations about triggers help partners recognize when the other acts from fear rather than malice.

Therapist Baum advises couples mid-argument to:

• Cool off and return regulated.
• Use "I statements" without blame.
• Listen reflectively — mirror words before responding.
• Validate feelings even when disagreeing on facts.

Trust takes time. After her first marriage ended, Baum told her second partner full trust would require five years. Through consistency and communication they built genuine security.

We will know we are moving into more healing when we have a different response to the old feelings arising. ~ Jessica Baum

Baum changed her attachment style, and research supports that possibility. Neuroplasticity allows attachment systems to shift through new, supportive experiences. Safety, empathy, and mutual vulnerability gradually build security.

Security is not the absence of conflict. It is the presence of repair, patience, and partners willing to keep showing up.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
If you thought "that's me" at any point, do not panic. Everyone carries relational baggage from the past. The encouraging news: you are not alone.

Turning inward to heal makes you more self-full and ready for genuinely secure relationships. This is not fantasy — many people have shifted attachment styles and built stronger bonds. You can too.

Try this

• Reflect on your attachment style and triggers.
• Identify your Inner Protector's voice, then recall Inner Nurturers you have internalized.
• Write relationship non-negotiables.
• Practice heart awareness when emotions spike.

Read it till the end?

You can rewrite old patterns. Healing attachment is slow work, but secure love is absolutely reachable.
